title: Module Knowledge Check: Clinical Examination of Hair and Scalp

items.stem: According to Volume 2 Module 5, which bedside clue most strongly supports miniaturisation rather than pure telogen shedding?

options.label: Marked calibre variability and mixed terminal and miniaturised hairs in patterned zones
Uniform-calibre short broken hairs without club roots
Shed telogen hairs with depigmented club roots only

items.rationale: The manual emphasises calibre variability as a key clue to miniaturisation; uniform short broken hairs suggest breakage; club roots typify shed telogen hairs.

items.stem: Outline a standard photography documentation approach for patterned hair loss and name two clinical features that should trigger escalation beyond reassurance-only pattern loss management.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Photography: fixed lighting and distance, reproducible views (e.g. frontal, frontal oblique, vertex, crown, lateral, donor, part-line close-ups as relevant), consistent hair preparation. Escalation examples: loss of follicular ostia or scarring clues; scalp pain, pustulation, or marked inflammatory signs; rapid progression without plausible trigger; systemic symptoms with diffuse shedding; patchy eyebrow or body hair loss.

items.rationale: Aligned to Volume 2 Module 5 sections on photography/grading and red flags.

items.stem: Which trichoscopic theme best supports a diagnosis of pattern hair loss in affected zones compared with a suitable control area?

options.label: Increased shaft diameter diversity and a higher proportion of thin or vellus-like hairs with reduced hairs per follicular unit
Preservation of shaft diversity with no miniaturisation gradient between frontal and occipital scalp in all cases
Yellow dots as the only finding, with no clinical correlation required

items.rationale: Volume 2 describes diameter diversity, miniaturised hairs, and reduced hairs per unit as supportive of pattern loss; interpretation is comparative and contextual.

items.stem: Explain why trichoscopy is valuable for biopsy site selection in suspected scarring alopecia and what types of findings would increase urgency for histology.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Trichoscopy maps active disease and structural loss (e.g. loss of ostia, white scar-like areas, tufting, casts, pustulation) and helps target an active advancing margin rather than end-stage scar. Findings suggesting cicatricial or destructive folliculitis increase urgency because delay can mean irreversible loss.

title: Module Knowledge Check: Investigations and Biopsy Logic

items.stem: When is broad androgen testing most justified in a woman with patterned hair loss according to the manual’s phenotype-led approach?

options.label: In every woman with central part widening regardless of history or examination
When there are hyperandrogenic or endocrine clues such as irregular cycles, hirsutism, severe acne, rapid severe onset, or infertility context
Only after hair transplant has been completed

items.rationale: Module 7 stresses targeted testing when clinical clues exist; not all FPHL requires androgen panels.

items.stem: A patient has diagnostic uncertainty, symptoms suggesting possible cicatricial disease, and trichoscopic loss of ostia at the margin of a patch. Outline your biopsy decision, preferred site philosophy, and what clinical information you send to pathology.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Biopsy is indicated due to diagnostic uncertainty and scarring concern. Site: active symptomatic margin with trichoscopic activity rather than burnt-out centre. Accompanying information: time course, symptoms, differentials, trichoscopic summary, prior treatments, and whether horizontal/vertical sections are preferred locally—invite clinicopathological correlation.

title: Module Knowledge Check: Androgenetic Alopecia in Males

items.stem: Which scenario most strongly challenges uncomplicated male AGA surgical candidacy?

options.label: Stable Norwood pattern with preserved donor characteristics and aligned expectations
Diffuse thinning extending into the traditionally safer donor zone or unstable donor miniaturisation
Mild frontal recession with strong family history

items.rationale: Module 8 highlights donor-zone involvement and instability as major caution points for surgery.

items.stem: Describe how you would counsel a man on stabilisation versus regrowth expectations when discussing finasteride and minoxidil (topical or oral where appropriate), without guaranteeing outcomes.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: Explain AGA as chronic miniaturisation needing long-term maintenance; finasteride targets DHT pathway in appropriate candidates; minoxidil supports growth calibre in responders; timelines are months; goals may be stabilisation, partial regrowth, or reduced progression—document shared decision-making, side-effect discussion, and that surgery is not a substitute for medical planning.

items.rationale: Module 8 medical ladder and counselling themes.

title: Module Knowledge Check: Female Pattern and Hormonal Hair Loss

items.stem: Which statement best reflects the manual’s teaching on female pattern hair loss and serum androgens?

options.label: Normal androgens exclude hormonally responsive follicular miniaturisation
Follicular sensitivity and phenotype can matter even when routine androgen measures are not elevated
Hyperandrogenism is present in all cases of FPHL

items.rationale: Module 9 stresses that normal serum androgens do not exclude responsive miniaturisation; not all FPHL is hyperandrogenic.

items.stem: Outline how you differentiate chronic telogen effluvium from female pattern hair loss in practice, naming at least two history elements and two examination/trichoscopy themes.

correctAnswer.freeTextSample: History: time course, triggers, postpartum, thyroid/iron/nutrition, medication, systemic symptoms, androgenic symptoms. Examination/trichoscopy: occipital comparison, pattern of miniaturisation and shaft diameter diversity in FPHL versus relative diversity preservation in acute TE (with caveats for mixed disease); part-line change; exclude inflammatory/scarring clues. Often requires integrated assessment rather than a single sign.
